_note: The Gym manifest (WP25a): which grammar points act as selectable form categories, grouped into picker columns. 'point' must match the grammar point title exactly (resolved to ids at request time). 'usage' is the plain-language hover explanation; 'example' is a real drill sentence from that point. 'nonstandard': entries hidden behind the 'include non-standard words' toggle.

entries.label: Present (polite)
entries.usage: What happens now or regularly — the everyday 요-form.
entries.example: 저는 학교에 가요. — I go to school.

entries.label: Past
entries.usage: What already happened: 았/었 slides in before the ending.
entries.example: 어제 학교에 갔어요. — I went to school yesterday.

entries.label: Future
entries.usage: Plans and predictions with (으)ㄹ 거예요.
entries.example: 내일 학교에 갈 거예요. — I will go to school tomorrow.

entries.label: Formal ㅂ니다
entries.usage: The news-and-business register: 갑니다, 먹습니다.
entries.example: 저는 매일 회사에 갑니다. — I go to the office every day.

entries.point: Ongoing and resulting: 고 있어요 / 아·어 있어요 (자고 있어요)
entries.label: Progressive
entries.usage: In the middle of doing: 고 있어요.
entries.example: 지금 밥을 먹고 있어요. — I'm eating right now.

entries.label: Can / can't
entries.usage: Ability and impossibility with (으)ㄹ 수 있어요/없어요.
entries.example: 저는 한국어를 할 수 있어요. — I can speak Korean.

entries.point: Must and may: 아/어야 해요, 아/어도 돼요 (가야 해요)
entries.label: Must / may
entries.usage: Obligation (야 해요) and permission (도 돼요).
entries.example: 지금 집에 가야 해요. — I have to go home now.

entries.point: Irregular verbs: ㅂ, ㄷ, 르 (추워요, 들어요, 몰라요)
entries.label: Irregular stems
entries.usage: The ㅂ/ㄷ/르 families transform before vowel endings: 춥다 → 추워요.
entries.example: 오늘 날씨가 정말 추워요. — The weather is really cold today.

entries.point: Honorific machinery: 께서, 드리다, 뵙다 (선생님께서 말씀하셨어요)
entries.label: Honorific forms
entries.usage: Raising the subject with 께서 + 시, humbling yourself with 드리다/뵙다.
entries.example: 선생님께서 오셨어요. — The teacher has arrived.

entries.label: Topic 은/는
entries.usage: What the sentence is about: 는 after vowels, 은 after consonants.
entries.example: 저는 학생이에요. — I am a student.

entries.label: Subject 이/가
entries.usage: Who or what does it — new information takes 이/가.
entries.example: 친구가 와요. — A friend is coming.

entries.point: Object particle 을/를 and verb-last order (밥을 먹어요)
entries.label: Object 을/를
entries.usage: The thing acted on: 를 after vowels, 을 after consonants.
entries.example: 밥을 먹어요. — I eat rice.

entries.point: Place and time: 에 vs 에서 (집에, 집에서)
entries.label: 에 vs 에서
entries.usage: Destination/time/being-there = 에; where an action happens = 에서.
entries.example: 도서관에서 공부해요. — I study at the library.

entries.label: 하고 / 도
entries.usage: 하고 joins nouns or means 'with'; 도 replaces the particle to say 'too'.
entries.example: 빵하고 우유를 샀어요. — I bought bread and milk.

entries.label: Comparison 보다
entries.usage: X보다 = more than X; 제일/가장 = the most.
entries.example: 형은 저보다 커요. — My brother is taller than me.

entries.label: Reason 아/어서
entries.usage: Reasons and lead-in sequences; no commands after it.
entries.example: 배가 고파서 밥을 먹었어요. — I was hungry, so I ate.

entries.label: Reason (으)니까
entries.usage: The reason connector that CAN introduce commands and suggestions.
entries.example: 시간이 없으니까 빨리 가세요. — There's no time, so go quickly.

entries.label: If (으)면
entries.usage: Conditions and habitual whens.
entries.example: 비가 오면 집에 있을 거예요. — If it rains, I'll stay home.

entries.point: But and and: 지만, 고 (춥지만, 먹고)
entries.label: But / and
entries.usage: 지만 contrasts; 고 lists and sequences.
entries.example: 날씨가 춥지만 갈 거예요. — It's cold, but I'll go.

entries.label: Noun modifiers
entries.usage: Whole clauses describe nouns from the front — Korean's relative clauses.
entries.example: 지금 읽는 책이 재미있어요. — The book I'm reading is interesting.

entries.label: Quotes 다고
entries.usage: Fold what someone said into your sentence.
entries.example: 친구가 내일 온다고 해요. — My friend says he's coming tomorrow.
